column1: // Golden cases for sqlparser in --sql mode (STEP 3: sql).
// Format: <input><TAB><expected AST>. Lines beginning with // are comments;
// blank lines are ignored. Expected value @ABORT means the input must make
// the parser exit nonzero.
// SQL targets the SAME (`verb; t; c; b; a) AST as ksql: t=from, c=where,
// b=group-by, a=select-list. Post-relational clauses (DISTINCT/ORDER BY/
// LIMIT) wrap the query noun as ordinary verbs.
// ---- the core equivalence: SELECT * is `all columns`, i.e. empty a ----
SELECT * FROM t
select * from t
// ---- keywords are case-insensitive; names keep their case ----
SeLeCt A FROM T
// ---- a select list, comma-separated ----
SELECT a, b FROM t
// ---- WHERE is an ordinary expression; one condition fills c ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE d > 0
// ---- top-level AND flattens into the c-list, like ksql's `where d>0,e<5` ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE d > 0 AND e < 5
// ---- OR stays a single combined phrase (& and | are the K and/or verbs) ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE d > 0 OR e < 5
// ---- AND binds tighter than OR (precedence, which K has no notion of) ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE a OR b AND c
// ---- aliases are the `:` assignment shape; AS reverses ksql's qty:expr ----
SELECT sum(amt) AS qty FROM t
// ---- the bare `expr name` alias form also works ----
SELECT sum(amt) qty FROM t
// ---- function calls are the K application shape (`fn;arg;...) ----
SELECT coalesce(a, b) FROM t
// ---- GROUP BY fills the b slot ----
SELECT a, b FROM t GROUP BY c
// ---- a fuller query: aliases, two conditions, two group keys ----
SELECT sum(i) AS p, avg(j) AS q FROM t WHERE f = c AND h > 5 GROUP BY f, g
// ---- arithmetic is left-associative (x-y-z is (x-y)-z, the opposite of K) ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE x - y - z > 0
// ---- SQL `/` (divide) maps to K `%`; two-char comparisons are sym heads ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE x / y > 1
SELECT a FROM t WHERE x <> 0
SELECT a FROM t WHERE x >= 1 AND x <= 9
// ---- NOT is looser than comparison: NOT a = b is NOT(a=b) ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE NOT a = b
// ---- post-relational clauses wrap the query as ordinary K verbs ----
// (asc/dsc are kparc's dyadic sort verbs; # take; _ drop; ?: unique)
SELECT a FROM t ORDER BY x
SELECT a FROM t ORDER BY x DESC
SELECT a FROM t ORDER BY x, y
SELECT a FROM t LIMIT 10
SELECT a FROM t LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5
SELECT DISTINCT a FROM t
// ---- wrappers compose in SQL's logical order: DISTINCT, then ORDER, then LIMIT ----
SELECT DISTINCT a FROM t ORDER BY x LIMIT 10
// ---- UPDATE: SET assignments fill a (the alias shape); b is always () ----
UPDATE t SET a = x + 1 WHERE d > 0
UPDATE t SET a = 1, b = 2 FROM_NOWHERE
UPDATE t SET a = 1, b = 2
// ---- DELETE only ever drops rows (fills c) ----
DELETE FROM t WHERE d > 0
DELETE FROM t
// ---- parens keep the OR as one phrase; the outer top-level AND still splits ----
SELECT a FROM t WHERE (a OR b) AND c
// ---- malformed: a written clause must carry its operand ----
SELECT a
SELECT FROM t
SELECT a FROM
SELECT a FROM t WHERE
SELECT a FROM t GROUP BY
// ---- malformed: clauses out of order leave a token dangling after the query ----
SELECT a FROM t GROUP BY c WHERE d
SELECT a FROM t LIMIT 10 ORDER BY x
// ---- malformed: only SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE start a statement ----
INSERT INTO t
